title: North Korean UNC1069 (Sapphire Sleet) Contagious Interview Expansion: 1,700 Malicious Packages Distributed Across npm, PyPI, Go, Rust, and PHP Ecosystems via Developer Social Engineering — WAVESHAPER.V2 RAT Harvests Cloud Credentials and CI/CD Pipeline Secrets at Scale
affected_products: npm (JavaScript) ecosystem — malicious packages including dev-log-core, logger-base, logkitx, pino-debugger
PyPI (Python) ecosystem — malicious packages including logutilkit, apachelicense, fluxhttp, license-utils-kit
Go Modules ecosystem — malicious packages impersonating Go standard libraries
Rust crates.io ecosystem — malicious package logtrace
PHP Packagist ecosystem — malicious utility packages
Development environments and CI/CD pipelines using affected packages

timeline.event: North Korean UNC1069 compromises npm maintainer account for Axios (100M+ weekly downloads) via social engineering; malicious versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 published containing WAVESHAPER.V2 RAT; approximately 600,000 installations occur within 3 hours before npm security team detects and removes them
npm security team removes malicious Axios versions; Microsoft Security Blog and Huntress publish initial mitigation guidance; confirmed 135+ organizations infected globally
Tenable and SecurityOnline publish scope and impact analysis; Elastic Security Labs begins forensic analysis of WAVESHAPER.V2 malware architecture and C2 infrastructure
Elastic Security Labs publishes detailed technical analysis of WAVESHAPER.V2 C++ backdoor including C2 communication patterns and credential harvesting capabilities

Unit 42 (Palo Alto Networks) publishes attribution of the Axios attack to North Korean UNC1069 with high confidence; Security Alliance blocks 164 UNC1069-linked domains impersonating Microsoft Teams and Zoom
Google Cloud Threat Intelligence formally attributes the Axios campaign to UNC1069 (Sapphire Sleet) with supporting technical evidence

Socket.dev discloses coordinated expansion of the campaign: UNC1069 has now distributed approximately 1,700 malicious packages across npm, PyPI, Go Modules, Rust crates.io, and PHP Packagist, all serving as staged loaders for WAVESHAPER.V2; The Hacker News and SOCRadar report on the multi-ecosystem scale

simple_summary.what_happened: North Korea's state-sponsored hacking group UNC1069 (also tracked as Sapphire Sleet, operating under the 'Contagious Interview' campaign) dramatically escalated their software supply chain attacks. Beginning with the compromise of the Axios npm package on March 31 — one of the most downloaded JavaScript libraries with over 100 million weekly users — the group subsequently distributed approximately 1,700 malicious packages across five major software package registries: npm (JavaScript), PyPI (Python), Go Modules, Rust (crates.io), and PHP (Packagist). Each malicious package silently installs a backdoor called WAVESHAPER.V2 on developer machines, giving North Korean operators the ability to steal cloud credentials, cryptocurrency, API keys, and sensitive source code.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Software developers, DevOps engineers, and organizations whose development environments, CI/CD pipelines, or production applications depend on affected packages across all five ecosystems. Organizations that installed Axios npm versions 1.14.1 or 0.30.4 during the March 31 window are confirmed potentially compromised. The campaign specifically targets technology companies, cryptocurrency firms, and organizations with significant cloud infrastructure.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: UNC1069 operators conduct targeted social engineering campaigns against open-source software maintainers, impersonating recruiters from cryptocurrency companies and AI firms to steal account credentials. Step 2: With compromised maintainer credentials, attackers publish malicious new versions of legitimate packages — or publish new packages designed to impersonate popular utilities (dev-log-core, logutilkit, logtrace, etc.). Step 3: Developers and automated CI/CD systems download the malicious packages believing them to be legitimate dependencies, integrating them into builds and pipelines. Step 4: The installed package executes a staged loader that downloads and installs the WAVESHAPER.V2 backdoor — a C++ RAT compiled to run as a persistent background daemon across Windows, macOS, and Linux. Step 5: WAVESHAPER.V2 harvests cloud credentials (AWS access keys, GitHub tokens, Kubernetes configs), SSH keys, cryptocurrency wallet files, and environment variables from the developer's machine. Step 6: Stolen secrets are transmitted to North Korean C2 servers via HTTP/HTTPS for use in cryptocurrency theft and espionage; some variants self-propagate by attempting to compromise additional package registry accounts found on the infected machine.
what_you_should_do: Immediately audit all npm, PyPI, Go, Rust, and PHP package dependency trees for known malicious packages: dev-log-core, logger-base, logkitx, pino-debugger, debug-fmt (npm); logutilkit, apachelicense, fluxhttp, license-utils-kit (PyPI); logtrace (crates.io) — remove and replace any found

If your environment installed Axios npm versions 1.14.1 or 0.30.4 at any point after March 31, 2026, treat all cloud credentials, API keys, SSH keys, GitHub/npm tokens, and Kubernetes configs on those machines as compromised — rotate them all immediately

Scan developer workstations and CI/CD pipeline nodes for WAVESHAPER.V2 behavioral indicators: persistent daemon processes spawned by package managers, periodic HTTP POST connections to external IPs from npm/pip runner processes, and unexpected access to ~/.aws/credentials or ~/.ssh/

Enable npm package signature verification (npm audit signatures) and integrate a Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tool such as Socket.dev, Snyk, or GitHub Dependabot to continuously monitor for newly identified malicious packages in your dependency trees
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: Software supply chain attacks on package registries represent one of the highest-leverage attack vectors available to nation-state actors. By compromising the Axios library — with 100M+ weekly downloads — a single malicious release reached hundreds of thousands of developer environments in hours. The 1,700-package expansion across 5 ecosystems demonstrates that UNC1069 has industrialized supply chain compromise into a systematic pipeline: targeting maintainers via fake job offers, publishing malicious packages, and harvesting credentials at scale. North Korea uses proceeds from cryptocurrency theft to directly fund its weapons programs, making these attacks a national security concern beyond the immediate cybersecurity impact. Compromised CI/CD pipelines mean attackers can pivot from developer laptops directly to production cloud infrastructure.

tactics.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
tactics.description: UNC1069 compromises legitimate package maintainer accounts via social engineering, then publishes malicious package versions to trusted registries (npm, PyPI, crates.io, Go Modules, Packagist)
Highly targeted social engineering campaigns impersonating cryptocurrency and AI company recruiters to steal package maintainer credentials via fake interview processes
Malicious packages act as staged loaders that fetch and install the WAVESHAPER.V2 C++ backdoor payload from attacker-controlled infrastructure upon execution during package installation
WAVESHAPER.V2 harvests AWS credential files (~/.aws/credentials), GitHub tokens, SSH private keys, and cloud API keys stored in common developer filesystem locations
WAVESHAPER.V2 establishes persistence as a daemon process via fork(), maintaining access to infected developer environments across reboots and sessions
Stolen credentials, source code, and environment data are exfiltrated via HTTP/HTTPS to North Korean C2 servers using the libcurl library; communication mimics legitimate developer tooling traffic

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying presence of known malicious package names in dependency trees, anomalous network connections from package manager processes, WAVESHAPER.V2 behavioral indicators such as persistent daemon creation and credential file access patterns, and unusual cloud API activity from developer credentials.

log_sources.source: Software Composition Analysis (SCA) / Dependency Audit Tools
log_sources.description: Continuously monitor package dependency trees for known malicious package names and versions; platforms like Socket.dev, Snyk, and GitHub Dependabot can flag compromised packages in real time

Monitor npm install, pip install, go get, cargo add, and composer install commands in CI/CD workflows for installation of suspicious packages, particularly recently published packages from unknown maintainers

Monitor for npm, pip, go, and cargo processes spawning unexpected persistent child processes, making outbound HTTP connections to external servers, or accessing credential files in the user home directory

log_sources.source: Cloud Platform Audit Logs (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Monitor, GCP Audit Logs)
log_sources.description: Monitor for API key usage from unexpected IP addresses, geographic locations, or at unusual times that may indicate use of stolen developer credentials harvested by WAVESHAPER.V2

what_to_look_for.indicator: Known malicious npm packages present in node_modules
what_to_look_for.description: Presence of dev-log-core, logger-base, logkitx, pino-debugger, debug-fmt, or debug-glitz in node_modules or package-lock.json indicates potential WAVESHAPER.V2 infection vector
Presence of logutilkit, apachelicense, fluxhttp, or license-utils-kit in pip environments; combined with logtrace in Rust environments
These specific Axios versions are the known-malicious releases published by UNC1069 on March 31, 2026; any installation of these versions should be treated as a confirmed compromise indicator

what_to_look_for.indicator: Package manager process accessing ~/.aws/credentials or SSH keys
what_to_look_for.description: npm, pip, go, or cargo processes accessing ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, or ~/.gitconfig is anomalous and indicates active credential harvesting by a malicious package
what_to_look_for.log_field: file_path CONTAINS '/.aws/credentials' OR '/.ssh/id_rsa' AND process_name IN ['node','npm','python3','pip','go','cargo']

immediate_actions.action: Audit all package dependencies for known malicious packages
immediate_actions.description: Immediately scan all active projects, CI/CD pipelines, and developer machines for the known malicious packages. For npm: dev-log-core, logger-base, logkitx, pino-debugger, and axios versions 1.14.1 or 0.30.4. For PyPI: logutilkit, apachelicense, fluxhttp, license-utils-kit. For Rust: logtrace.
immediate_actions.command:
# Check npm for malicious packages:
npm ls dev-log-core logger-base logkitx pino-debugger 2>/dev/null
# Check specific malicious Axios versions:
npm ls axios 2>/dev/null | grep -E '1\.14\.1|0\.30\.4'
# Check PyPI:
pip show logutilkit apachelicense fluxhttp license-utils-kit 2>/dev/null

immediate_actions.action: Rotate all credentials on machines that installed affected packages
immediate_actions.description: For any machine that installed axios 1.14.1/0.30.4 or the known malicious packages, immediately rotate: AWS IAM access keys, GitHub/GitLab personal access tokens, npm/PyPI publish tokens, SSH keys, Kubernetes kubeconfig credentials, and any other secrets stored in the developer's home directory or CI/CD environment variables.
immediate_actions.command:
# List and rotate AWS access keys for affected IAM users:
aws iam list-access-keys --user-name [username]
aws iam delete-access-key --access-key-id [old-key-id] --user-name [username]
aws iam create-access-key --user-name [username]

immediate_actions.action: Hunt for WAVESHAPER.V2 on developer endpoints and CI/CD nodes
immediate_actions.description: Use EDR tools to search for persistent background processes spawned by package manager processes, unusual periodic HTTP POST traffic from development tools to external IPs, and access to credential files (~/.aws/, ~/.ssh/) by unexpected processes. Review all process trees from npm/pip/go/cargo install commands since March 31, 2026.

immediate_actions.action: Enable package signing verification and SCA tooling organization-wide
immediate_actions.description: Enable npm package provenance and signature verification and integrate a Software Composition Analysis tool (Socket.dev, Snyk, GitHub Dependabot Alerts) that detects newly identified malicious packages. Configure CI/CD pipelines to fail on packages flagged by SCA tools.
immediate_actions.command:
# Enable npm audit with signature verification:
npm audit signatures
# Run npm audit in CI/CD at moderate level:
npm audit --audit-level=moderate
